metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Components - Tabs

header.title: Tabs
header.description: Tabs organize content across different screens and views

items: Use tabs to group content into helpful categories
Tabs can horizontally scroll, so a UI can have as many tabs as needed
Place tabs next to each other as peers

data.resource: Figma: Atmosphere Foundations and Components Library

sections.text:
Measurements

Tabs come in two density modes: relaxed and condensed.

Usage

Tabs organize groups of related content that are at the same level of hierarchy.

Use tabs to group related content, not sequential content.

Container

The container holds multiple tabs. Its contents can be fixed or scrollable. It should always extend the full width of the window/pane and its tabs can be divided into equal sections or be left or center aligned.

A divider on the bottom edge separates it from the content below. Content may scroll under the container.

Icon (optional)

Icons communicate the type of content within a tab. Icons should be simple and recognizable. Tab content should not be represented with icons alone, as an icon’s meaning may not be clear.

Label

Text labels should clearly describe the content within the tab. They should be short and appear in a single row.

Badge (optional)

Tabs can use a large or small badge next to the tab label to show notifications, counts, or status of the information contained inside the tab content.

Placement

Tabs are displayed in a single row, with each tab connected to the content it represents. As a set, all tabs are unified by a shared topic.

Main tabs should always be placed at the top of the content pane or under a top app bar. If there’s a need for a second level of tabs they will be used within a content area to further separate related content and establish hierarchy.

images.text: Tabs as a second level of navigation
Don`t use two tab menus next to each other or right after a top app bar horizontal menu

1. Inactive enabled
  2. Inactive hovered
  3. Inactive pressed
  4. Active enabled

  6. Active pressed

sections.text:
Fixed tabs

Fixed tabs display all tabs in a set simultaneously. They are best for switching between related content quickly. Navigate to a tab by tapping on it.

These tabs can be divided into equal sections or be left or center aligned.

sections.textBefore:
Scrollable tabs

When a set of tabs cannot fit on screen, scrollable tabs are used. They are best used for browsing on mobile devices and  can use longer text labels and a larger number of tabs.

It's not recommended to loop a tab set where it scrolls infinitely. This can trap users who are navigating linearly with a screen reader.
sections.textAfter:
The content will scroll horizontally under the arrows, while pressing the arrow buttons on the left/right sides of the tab menu.

If one side is showing the first/last tab, the nearest arrow will become disabled.

sections.text:
Scrolling content

When a screen scrolls up and down through content, tabs will be fixed to the top of the screen/pane.

Cursor

When hovered, the hover state appears, providing a visual cue that the tab is interactive. When clicked (in both active and inactive states), a ripple appears and the indicator shifts into position, showing the user feedback.

To navigate between scrollable tabs, users must click the left or right arrow and click to select an individual tab.

Keyboard

On arrow/tab in a tab menu, the active indicator appears on the first interactive element, providing feedback to the user that it is selected. The user is then able to tab to additional interactive elements until all available items are complete within the tab menu.

Users should be able to navigate to and activate a tab inside a tab menu with these keys:

data.actions: Focus on the next available enabled item
Allows navigation through tab menu items

sections.text:
Using labels

Some accessibility labels require more descriptive text, particularly when the visible UI is ambiguous or not visible. 

For full-width tabs, the width of each tab will be divided into equal sections, aligning the label in the center. For left or center aligned tabs, the width for each tab will be different, depending on the label. In this case, a max tab width of 300px-350px is reccommended.

Don’t truncate labels unless required, as truncated text can impede comprehension. In this case, the label will be truncated with an ellipsis and supported by a tooltip.

Responsive behavior

When a set of tabs cannot fit on screen, as in small devices or if the viewport size is reduced, they will become scrollable tabs.

Densities

The tab menu and the icons within will be reduced in height for condensed versions. Mobile screens should always use relaxed density to ensure touch accessibility. All of these changes can be visually seen in the Measurements section.
